section.title: Sodom Destroyed
section.sectionRationale: The judgment Abraham had pleaded against now falls. The chapter exposes the depth of Sodom's wickedness, the reluctant rescue of Lot by grace alone, and the sobering aftermath in the cave. It stands throughout Scripture as a fixed warning of judgment and a display of mercy that saves the undeserving.
section.thesis: God's patience with wickedness has a limit, and when it comes His judgment is real and total; yet He rescues Lot not for Lot's worthiness but because He remembered Abraham, showing that salvation from wrath is always by grace.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Lot's dangerous hospitality and the assault on his house; the urgent flight from the doomed city; the fire from heaven and Lot's wife; and the grim aftermath in the cave.

verses.text: Now the two {{malak|angels}} arrived at Sodom in the evening, and Lot was sitting in the gateway of the city. When Lot saw them, he got up to meet them, bowed facedown,

and said, “My lords, please turn aside into the house of your servant; wash your feet and spend the night. Then you can rise early and go on your way.” “No,” they answered, “we will spend the night in the square.”
But Lot insisted so strongly that they followed him into his house. He prepared a feast for them and baked unleavened bread, and they ate.
Before they had gone to bed, all the men of the city of Sodom, both young and old, surrounded the house.
They called out to Lot, saying, “Where are the men who came to you tonight? Send them out to us so we can have relations with them!”
Lot went outside to meet them, shutting the door behind him.
“Please, my brothers,” he pleaded, “don’t do such a wicked thing!

Look, I have two daughters who have never slept with a man. Let me bring them to you, and you can do to them as you please. But do not do anything to these men, for they have come under the protection of my roof.”

“Get out of the way!” they replied. And they declared, “This one came here as a foreigner, and he is already acting like a judge! Now we will treat you worse than them.” And they pressed in on Lot and moved in to break down the door.
But the men inside reached out, pulled Lot into the house with them, and shut the door.
And they struck the men at the entrance, young and old, with blindness, so that they wearied themselves trying to find the door.

units.label: Flee, and do not look back

verses.text: Then the two men said to Lot, “Do you have anyone else here, a son-in-law, your sons or daughters, or anyone else in the city who belongs to you? Get them out of here,
because we are about to destroy this place. For the {{outcry|outcry}} to the LORD against its people is so great that He has sent us to destroy it.”

So Lot went out and spoke to the sons-in-law who were pledged in marriage to his daughters. “Get up,” he said. “Get out of this place, for the LORD is about to destroy the city!” But his sons-in-law thought he was joking.
At daybreak the angels hurried Lot along, saying, “Get up! Take your wife and your two daughters who are here, or you will be swept away in the punishment of the city.”
But when Lot hesitated, the men grabbed his hand and the hands of his wife and his two daughters. And they led them safely out of the city, because of the LORD’s {{chemlah|compassion}} for them.
As soon as the men had brought them out, one of them said, “Run for your lives! Do not look back, and do not stop anywhere on the plain! Flee to the mountains, or you will be swept away!”
But Lot replied, “No, my lords, please!
Your servant has indeed found favor in your sight, and you have shown me great kindness by sparing my life. But I cannot run to the mountains; the disaster will overtake me, and I will die.
Look, there is a town nearby where I can flee, and it is a small place. Please let me flee there, is it not a small place? Then my life will be saved.”
“Very well,” he answered, “I will grant this request as well, and will not demolish the town you indicate.
Hurry! Run there quickly, for I cannot do anything until you reach it.” That is why the town was called Zoar.
And by the time the sun had risen over the land, Lot had reached Zoar.
Then the LORD rained down sulfur and fire on Sodom and Gomorrah, from the LORD out of the heavens.
Thus He destroyed these cities and the entire plain, including all the inhabitants of the cities and everything that grew on the ground.
But Lot’s wife looked back, and she became a pillar of salt.
Early the next morning, Abraham got up and returned to the place where he had stood before the LORD.
He looked down toward Sodom and Gomorrah and all the land of the plain, and he saw the smoke rising from the land like smoke from a furnace.
So when God destroyed the cities of the plain, He {{zakar|remembered}} Abraham, and He brought Lot out of the catastrophe that destroyed the cities where he had lived.
Lot and his two daughters left Zoar and settled in the mountains, for he was afraid to stay in Zoar, where they lived in a cave.
One day the older daughter said to the younger, “Our father is old, and there is no man in the land to sleep with us, as is the custom over all the earth.
Come, let us get our father drunk with wine so we can sleep with him and preserve his line.”
So that night they got their father drunk with wine, and the firstborn went in and slept with her father; he was not aware when she lay down or when she got up.

The next day the older daughter said to the younger, “Look, I slept with my father last night. Let us get him drunk with wine again tonight so you can go in and sleep with him and we can preserve our father’s line.”
So again that night they got their father drunk with wine, and the younger daughter went in and slept with him; he was not aware when she lay down or when she got up.
Thus both of Lot’s daughters became pregnant by their father.
The older daughter gave birth to a son and named him Moab. He is the father of the Moabites of today.
The younger daughter also gave birth to a son, and she named him Ben-ammi. He is the father of the Ammonites of today.

malak.gloss: A messenger or angel. The two who were with the LORD at Mamre now come to Sodom as agents of both rescue and judgment. They deliver Lot and then destroy the city, showing that the same visitation brings salvation to some and ruin to others.

outcry.gloss: A cry, an outcry, often the scream of the oppressed for help. Sodom's sin has produced such a cry to the LORD that judgment is sent. The word links Sodom's violence to the suffering it caused and shows that God hears and answers the cries of the wronged.

chemlah.gloss: Compassion, pity, sparing mercy. Lot is saved not because he is bold or ready, but because of the LORD's compassion; the angels seize his hand while he lingers. It names the tender pity of God that rescues the hesitant and undeserving.

zakar.gloss: To remember, to call to mind and act upon. God remembered Abraham, and so Lot was brought out. To remember in Scripture is not mere recollection but faithful action; Lot's rescue is credited to God honoring His covenant friend's intercession.

basic.context: The two angels who left Abraham arrive in Sodom, and the city's wickedness is laid bare in the mob at Lot's door. The judgment Abraham pleaded against now falls. The key question is why Lot is saved at all, and the answer is grace: God remembered Abraham.
basic.hermeneutics: This is sober history, and Scripture treats it as a fixed example of judgment and of rescue. The narrative describes terrible things, including Lot's offer of his daughters and the later incest, without approving them. The debate is over Lot's spiritual state, but the clear point is that salvation from wrath is by mercy, not merit.
basic.originalLanguages: The two are called angels, messengers who both rescue and destroy. Sodom's sin has raised an outcry, the cry of the oppressed, to the LORD. Lot is led out because of God's compassion, His sparing pity, and above all because God remembered Abraham, a remembering that always leads to action.
basic.typology: The fire that falls on Sodom becomes a standing picture of final judgment. Lot dragged out by the hand pictures salvation that grips the reluctant. Lot's wife looking back warns of a heart still bound to the world under judgment.
basic.christ: Jesus points to this day to warn of His coming: remember Lot's wife, and as it was in the days of Lot, so it will be. He is the greater rescuer who bears the fire of judgment Himself so His people can be brought out safely.
basic.god: God is holy and just, and His patience with evil is not endless. Yet He is compassionate, seizing the hand of a hesitating man, and faithful, remembering His covenant with Abraham even when saving Abraham's undeserving nephew.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Peter 2 and Jude cite Sodom as an example of judgment and Lot as rescued. Luke 17 has Jesus recall these days and Lot's wife. Deuteronomy remembers the overthrow of the cities.
basic.oneStory: Sodom shows that judgment is real and that rescue from it is by grace alone. Lot is saved because God remembered Abraham, a foretaste of how all who are saved are spared for the sake of Another. The story presses on toward Christ, who takes the fire so His people can flee to safety.

context.literary: The chapter mirrors and darkens chapter 18: two of the three visitors now arrive, and hospitality that honored Abraham's guests turns into a violent assault at Lot's door. The narrative races with imperatives, get up, hurry, flee, do not look back, then falls silent over the ruin and the cave.
context.historical: Sodom lay in the fertile plain Lot had chosen. Its destruction by sulfur and fire near the region of the Dead Sea became proverbial in Israel for total, irreversible judgment. Moab and Ammon, born in the cave, were neighboring peoples with a long and often hostile history with Israel.
context.canonical: Sodom is the Bible's recurring shorthand for wickedness ripe for judgment and for the certainty of divine wrath, cited by the prophets, by Jesus, and by Peter and Jude, who also hold up Lot's rescue as a pattern of God delivering the godly from trial.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To display the reality and totality of God's judgment on entrenched wickedness, to show that Lot's rescue rests on God's compassion and His remembrance of Abraham, and to sound a lasting warning against a heart still clinging to the doomed world.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates sins it does not endorse, Lot's offer of his daughters and the incest that follows; these are recorded, not commanded. The prescriptive truths are the certainty of judgment, the freeness of grace, and the danger of looking back.

debates.question: Was Lot a righteous man or a compromised one?

views.label: Righteous yet deeply compromised
views.case: Peter calls Lot a righteous man tormented by Sodom's lawlessness, 2 Peter 2:7-8, even though his choices, his lingering, and his family show a faith badly eroded by the city.

views.label: A cautionary figure of worldliness
views.case: The narrative highlights Lot's hesitation, his bargaining, and the ruin of his household as a warning of what it costs to pitch one's tent toward Sodom.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree Lot is saved by grace, not by his own goodness; his story warns against worldliness even as it displays God's mercy toward the undeserving.

study.originalLanguages: The two visitors are called malakim, angels or messengers, and the same pair both rescue Lot and destroy the city. Sodom's sin has raised a tseaqah, an outcry, the scream of the oppressed, before the LORD. Lot is led out because of God's chemlah, His sparing compassion, as the angels grip his hesitating hand. And the reason given for the whole rescue is that God zakar, remembered, Abraham; in Scripture such remembering is never bare recollection but faithful, saving action.

typology.type: Fire and sulfur falling on Sodom
typology.fulfillment: The picture of final judgment on the ungodly, Luke 17:29-30, 2 Peter 2:6, from which Christ delivers His people.

typology.type: Lot seized by the hand and dragged to safety
typology.fulfillment: Salvation as God's gracious grip on those who linger, rescuing the reluctant by mercy, Jude 23.

typology.type: Lot's wife turning back and becoming salt
typology.fulfillment: The warning of Christ, remember Lot's wife, against a heart still fixed on the world under judgment, Luke 17:32.

study.christ: Jesus reaches back to this day to warn of His own coming: as it was in the days of Lot, so will it be when the Son of Man is revealed, and remember Lot's wife, Luke 17:28-32. Sodom becomes the measure of judgment, and the gospel is that Christ bears that very fire of wrath Himself so that His people may be led out to safety. Lot, rescued not for his worth but because God remembered Abraham, is a picture of every believer spared not for their own merit but for the sake of Another, the true and better covenant Head.
study.god: God is revealed as holy and just: His patience with Sodom's entrenched evil finally reaches its limit, and His judgment is complete. Yet in the same chapter He is compassionate, His angels seizing the hand of a lingering man, and He is faithful, remembering His covenant with Abraham and for his sake delivering Lot. Judgment and mercy stand side by side, and mercy triumphs for those He rescues.

crossReferences.note: God made Sodom an example, yet rescued righteous Lot, showing He knows how to deliver the godly from trials.
Sodom and Gomorrah serve as an example of those who undergo the punishment of eternal fire.
Jesus recalls the days of Lot and warns, remember Lot's wife, to press readiness for His coming.
The judgment here answers Abraham's intercession; God spared no righteous city, but remembered Abraham and saved Lot.

study.oneStory: Sodom's overthrow proves that God's judgment on hardened wickedness is real and total, while Lot's rescue proves that deliverance from that judgment is by grace alone. Lot is saved because God remembered Abraham, a foretaste of how all the saved are spared for the sake of Another. The line runs on to Christ, who endures the fire of judgment so that His people may be led safely out.

quiz.q: Why was Lot rescued from the destruction of Sodom?

options.text: Because of God's compassion and because He remembered Abraham.
options.note: The rescue rests on grace and God's covenant with Abraham, not Lot's merit.

options.text: Because Lot was bold and ready to flee at once.
options.note: In fact Lot hesitated and had to be dragged out; the rescue was by mercy.

options.text: Because Lot had converted the city to righteousness.
options.note: The city was wholly given to wickedness; Lot saved none of it.

options.text: Because Lot bargained his way out with the angels.
options.note: He bargained about where to flee, but his deliverance came from God's compassion.

quiz.q: What happened to Lot's wife, and why does it matter?

options.text: She looked back and became a pillar of salt, a warning against clinging to the doomed world.
options.note: Jesus says, remember Lot's wife, Luke 17:32.

options.text: She was left behind in the city by accident.
options.note: She was brought out but looked back; it was not mere accident.

options.text: She refused to leave and stayed in Sodom.
options.note: She did leave, but turned back with her heart still in the city.

options.text: She led the family safely to the mountains.
options.note: She perished on the way; she did not lead them to safety.

quiz.q: How did the men of Sodom respond to Lot's visitors?

options.text: They surrounded the house and demanded to abuse the men.
options.note: Their assault exposes the wickedness that provoked judgment.

options.text: They welcomed the strangers with their own hospitality.
options.note: Only Lot showed hospitality; the city showed violent hostility.

options.text: They ignored the visitors entirely.
options.note: Far from ignoring them, they violently sought them out.

options.text: They repented when they heard of the coming judgment.
options.note: They hardened themselves and pressed in even after being struck blind.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament use the destruction of Sodom?

options.text: As an example of judgment on the ungodly and of God rescuing the godly.
options.note: 2 Peter 2 and Jude cite both the judgment and Lot's rescue.

options.text: As proof that God never sends judgment.
options.note: The opposite; it is the standard example that judgment is real.

options.text: As a myth with no bearing on later teaching.
options.note: Jesus, Peter, and Jude all treat it as real and instructive.

options.text: As evidence that the wicked are always spared.
options.note: The wicked were destroyed; only Lot's family was spared, by grace.

quiz.q: What does it mean that God remembered Abraham?

options.text: God acted on His covenant faithfulness, bringing Lot out for Abraham's sake.
options.note: Biblical remembering is faithful, saving action.

options.text: God suddenly recalled a promise He had forgotten.
options.note: God does not forget; remembering here means He acted on His covenant.

options.text: God rewarded Lot for his own righteousness.
options.note: The rescue is tied to Abraham, not to Lot's merit.

options.text: God changed His mind about judging Sodom.
options.note: Sodom was still destroyed; only Lot was brought out.

questions: The angels had to seize Lot's hand because he lingered. Where are you hesitating over something God is clearly calling you to leave behind?
Lot was saved because God remembered Abraham, not because Lot deserved it. How does it humble and comfort you to know that salvation is by grace, not merit?
Jesus said, 'Remember Lot's wife.' Is there something in the world under judgment that your heart keeps looking back to?
Sodom's sin raised an outcry that God heard and answered. How does it change your view of injustice to know that God hears the cries of the oppressed?
Lot pitched his tent toward Sodom and it cost his family dearly. What small compromises might be pulling your household in a direction you would not choose?

translationNotes.note: From the LORD out of the heavens follows the phrase set off by dashes in the printed text, rendered here with a comma; it stresses that the judgment came directly from God.
Zoar means small; the name preserves Lot's plea that the town was only a small place.
Moab sounds like the Hebrew for from my father, and Ben-ammi means son of my people; the names quietly mark the origin of these nations in the cave.
